AN NWS STORM SURVEY CONFIRMED WIDESPREAD WIND DAMAGE IN THE CITY OF CLARKSVILLE WAS DUE TO STRAIGHT LINE WINDS OF 70 TO 90 MPH PRODUCED BY A QUASI-LINEAR CONVECTIVE SYSTEM, OR SQUALL LINE. A VAST MAJORITY OF THE DAMAGE WAS TO THE EAST OR EAST NORTHEAST AND DID NOT SHOW SIGNS OF A CONVERGENT DAMAGE PATTERN. IN ADDITION, THE WIDTH OF THE WIND DAMAGE SWATH IN CLARKSVILLE WAS OVER A MILE IN PORTIONS OF THE CITY, WHICH DOES NOT CORRELATE WELL WITH DAMAGE PRODUCED BY A WEAK TORNADO. MANY TREES AND LARGE LIMBS WERE DOWNED BY THE WINDS WITH SOME STRUCTURAL DAMAGE IN THE CITY DUE TO FALLING TREES. HOWEVER, THERE WAS A LOT OF EVIDENCE OF WINDS ALONE DOING A VARIETY OF ROOF DAMAGE IN TOWN, WITH MANY RESIDENCES LOSING A SIGNIFICANT AMOUNT OF SHINGLES ON THEIR ROOFS. THERE WERE ALSO A FEW INSTANCES OF GLASS BEING BLOWN OUT OF WINDOWS IN SOME STRUCTURES AND ONE OR TWO MORE WEAKLY CONSTRUCTED BUILDINGS DID HAVE LARGE PORTIONS OF THEIR ROOF STRUCTURES BLOWN OFF.

AN EF-0 TORNADO WITH MAXIMUM ESTIMATED WINDS NEAR 80 MPH TOUCHED DOWN IN THE MIDTOWN AREA OF TYLER, NEAR LLOYD JAMES FUNERAL HOME AT THE INTERSECTION OF EAST 1ST STREET AND SOUTH FLEISHEL AVENUE, WHERE NUMEROUS SMALL AND LARGE LIMBS WERE SNAPPED OFF OF NEARBY TREES, AND A FEW SHINGLES WERE TORN OFF THE ROOF OF THE CHAPEL. IN ADDITION, A LARGE CANOPY ON THE FRONT ENTRANCE OF THE FUNERAL HOME WAS TORN OFF AND THROWN INTO A NEARBY TREE. THE TORNADO THEN MOVED JUST EAST OF THE UT HEALTH TYLER CAMPUS WHERE IT CROSSED EAST IDEL STREET, AND PEELED OFF A PORTION OF A METAL ROOF FROM A NEARBY MEDICAL OFFICE BUILDING. A GLASS CANOPY WAS PARTIALLY LIFTED UP AND SHATTERED IN FRONT OF THE MEDICAL OFFICE BUILDING, AND A NEARBY HARDWOOD TREE HAD SEVERAL LIMBS WHICH WERE SNAPPED. THE TORNADO CONTINUED NORTHEAST ALONG SOUTH FLEISHEL AVENUE, WHERE IT UPROOTED A HARDWOOD TREE WHICH DAMAGED THE CORNER OF A NEARBY RESIDENCE AT THE END OF WOODLEY STREET, AND ALSO SNAPPED LARGE LIMBS OFF OF A SOFTWOOD TREE ON EAST HOUSTON STREET BEFORE LIFTING. THE MAX PATH WIDTH WAS 65 YARDS, WITH A PATH LENGTH OF 0.70 MILES.

An EF-1 tornado formed along a strong line of thunderstorms moving across East Texas and Northwest Louisiana. The tornado first touched down in East Texas on County Road 322 in Panola County, where a large tree fell on a home. The tornado continued to move southeast for 7.78 miles, with a maximum width of 2100 yards, before lifting east of the Four Forks community in Southwest Caddo Parish in Louisiana. The majority of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted trees, with maximum winds of 110 mph.

AN EF-1 TORNADO FORMED ALONG A STRONG LINE OF THUNDERSTORMS MOVING ACROSS EAST TEXAS AND NORTHWEST LOUISIANA. THE TORNADO FIRST TOUCHED DOWN IN EAST TEXAS ON COUNTY ROAD 322 IN PANOLA COUNTY, WHERE A LARGE TREE FELL ON A HOME. THE TORNADO CONTINUED TO MOVE SOUTHEAST FOR 7.78 MILES, WITH A MAXIMUM WIDTH OF 844 YARDS, BEFORE LIFTING EAST OF THE FOUR FORKS COMMUNITY IN SOUTHWEST CADDO PARISH IN LOUISIANA. THE MAJORITY OF THE DAMAGE CONSISTED OF SNAPPED AND UPROOTED TREES, WITH MAXIMUM WINDS OF 110 MPH.

An NWS survey crew confirmed straight-line wind damage on the northwestern outskirts of Jacksonville TX, produced by a severe supercell thunderstorm. Most of the direct damage was to trees, although one heavily damaged metal building system garnered a peak wind estimate of 96 mph.

An EF-1 tornado with maximum estimated winds near 93 mph touched down just south of Stamps along McKamie Road between Lakeside Cemetery and Lake June. The tornado tracked south along and just east of McKamie Road/Highway 53, where multiple trees were snapped and uprooted as the it crossed Ferguson Hill Road and Hamilton Street. The tornado strengthened to its peak intensity as it crossed Fuller Drive south to CR 84, and continued south across CR 85 and CR 23 before lifting near Highway 53 just south of CR 23. The estimated path width of the tornado was 350 yards, with a path length of 2.9 miles.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 105 mph touched down in extreme Southern Natchitoches Parish in the Kisatchie National Forest along Forest Road 306, and tracked northeast across Highway 119 in the Gorum community. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted along its path, with the tornado crossing Andy Cedars Road, where a portion of a tin roof was ripped off of a home. The tornado crossed Janie-Gorum Road, where additional trees were snapped and uprooted, a tree fell onto a single family home, and a camper was blown over. Additional trees were downed and a roof was ripped off of a doublewide mobile home farther east along Janie-Gorum Road, before the tornado lifted near Sunk Hill Road. The estimated path length was 8.8 miles, with a path width of 150 yards.

Around 2:40 PM CST, NWS Doppler radar indicated a possible tornado on the ground, with a debris signature evident. This tornado produced structural damage and downed trees near the town of Gorum near and around HWY 119.
